feedingProfile.preferredNpk: balanced (e.g. 10-10-10) or bloom-booster higher-phosphorus liquid feed; slow-release fertilizer mixed into the potting mix at planting

commonIssues: Frost-tender annual in most climates (perennial only in USDA zones 10-11): plant out only after the last spring frost, once soil has warmed to about 15-16 C (60 F).
Too little sun makes plants spindly with few flowers; petunias need at least 5-6 hours of direct sun and bloom best in full sun all day.
Plants get leggy by midsummer; cut shoots back by about half, then feed and water to force fresh growth and bloom.
Deadhead spent flowers regularly (especially large-flowered types) to keep new buds forming.
Containers and hanging baskets dry out fast in summer and may need water daily, sometimes twice a day in heat; in-ground plants need far less (a deep weekly soak).
Avoid soggy soil and frequent shallow watering: poor drainage invites root rot and gray mold, and shallow watering encourages shallow roots.
Petal blight and fungal problems appear in rainy/very humid weather, especially on grandiflora types; avoid wetting the foliage.
Watch for aphids and slugs/snails; knock aphids off with water and handpick or trap slugs.
Spreading/trailing (e.g. Wave-type) petunias are heavier feeders and drinkers than standard types: feed weekly and water more often.

source.citation: University of Minnesota Extension, 'Growing petunias': at least 5-6 hours of good sunlight, best in full sun all day; in-ground plants need a thorough weekly watering soaking soil 6-8 inches deep, while container plantings may need daily watering; garden beds get balanced fertilizer (8-8-8, 10-10-10, or 12-12-12) at planting then liquid feed every ~3 weeks from July; containers get timed-release fertilizer at planting then flowering-plant liquid feed every 2 weeks (weekly for spreading types); transplant after frost danger when soil is about 60 F; start seed indoors 10-12 weeks before planting out.

University of Minnesota Extension, 'Growing petunias': container petunias may need daily watering in summer depending on pot size and soil volume; in-ground plants need a thorough weekly soak 6-8 inches deep; spreading types need frequent watering.

University of Minnesota Extension, 'Growing petunias': feed container petunias every 2 weeks with a flowering-plant liquid fertilizer (weekly for spreading types); timed-release fertilizer incorporated at planting; balanced 8-8-8 / 10-10-10 / 12-12-12 for beds.

Clemson University Home & Garden Information Center, 'Petunia' (HGIC): full sunlight required, plants become spindly with few flowers in shade; 1-2 inches of water every 7-10 days once established in ground, avoid frequent light watering; fertilize monthly with a balanced fertilizer; well-drained light soil of medium fertility, pH 6.0-7.0; heights 6-18 in, spread 18 in to 4 ft; excellent for containers and hanging baskets; aphids, slugs, and petal blight in rainy/humid weather are the main problems.
Clemson University HGIC 'Petunia': soil pH 6.0-7.0, well-drained light soil of medium fertility; excellent for containers and hanging baskets.

The Old Farmer's Almanac, 'Petunias: How to Plant, Grow, and Care for Petunias': annual in most areas, tender perennial only in zones 9-11; plant out after last spring frost and protect young plants from late frosts; full sun or plants become spindly; thorough weekly watering suffices in ground, but spreading types and container plants need more frequent watering; fertilize monthly with balanced fertilizer (biweekly for double-flowered cultivars); prune leggy plants back by half in midsummer; aphids and slugs are the main pests; fungal disease develops with excess moisture and poor airflow.

The Old Farmer's Almanac, 'Petunias': blooms from spring until frost; treated as annuals, tender perennial in zones 9-11; frost-sensitive — plant after last spring frost; height 6-18 in, spread 18 in to 4 ft; spreading/trailing types ideal for window boxes and hanging baskets.

Missouri Botanical Garden Plant Finder, 'Petunia (group)': winter hardy USDA zones 10-11, grown as an annual elsewhere; full sun to part shade; medium water; height 0.75-1 ft, spread 1-3 ft; blooms May to frost; average, medium-moisture, well-drained soil; cut back plants that decline in summer heat; susceptible to root rot, gray mold, late blight, tobacco mosaic virus; aphids, flea beetles, slugs and snails.

Royal Horticultural Society (RHS), Petunia genus page: grow in light, well-drained soil in full sun with shelter from drying winds; plant out after frosts have passed; sow seed at 13-18 C in early spring; flowers late spring to autumn.

Confidence note: container minimum liters (8 L recommended / 11-20 L ideal) estimated from common horticultural practice (a ~10-12 inch pot per petunia; 12 inch pot is roughly 11 L) because the extension sources reviewed give container guidance qualitatively rather than in volume units. heatStressC (32 C) is a conservative estimate derived from Missouri Botanical Garden's note that plants decline in summer heat and should be cut back; no reviewed source gives an exact heat-stress threshold. wateringProfile.baseIntervalDays of 1 reflects UMN/Almanac guidance that container petunias may need daily water in summer; in-ground plants need only a deep weekly soak.
